trade.summary: The US is Korea's second-largest trading partner. Korea runs a persistent trade surplus with the US, which has been a source of friction. The KORUS FTA (Korea-US Free Trade Agreement, 2012) governs the relationship. Major Korean exports include semiconductors (Samsung, SK Hynix), automobiles (Hyundai, Kia), ships, and electronics. US exports to Korea include aircraft, semiconductors (US-designed), agricultural products, and LNG. Korean companies have made major investments in US manufacturing in response to the Inflation Reduction Act.
disputes: US concern over Korean trade surplus
disputes over Korean automobile market access

diplomatic.summary: The US-Korea alliance is one of the cornerstones of Indo-Pacific security. Established by the US-ROK Mutual Defense Treaty (1953), the alliance includes ~28,500 US troops stationed in Korea. The 2023 Washington Declaration reaffirmed extended deterrence commitments including nuclear umbrella assurances in response to North Korea's nuclear advances. The 2023 Camp David trilateral with Japan represented a historic upgrade in trilateral security coordination. Korea's relationship with the US is its most important foreign relationship.
recent_incidents: 2023 Washington Declaration reaffirming nuclear extended deterrence
2023 Camp David trilateral with US and Japan
Ongoing USFK (US Forces Korea) host nation support negotiations
Korea's Inflation Reduction Act manufacturing investment response

military.summary: The US-Korea military alliance is one of the world's most integrated bilateral defense relationships. The US stations 28,500 troops in Korea and maintains Combined Forces Command (CFC) with a US general as commander. The two militaries conduct extensive joint exercises (Ulchi Freedom Shield, Foal Eagle). The US nuclear umbrella is central to deterring North Korea. Korea is acquiring US-made F-35 stealth fighters, Patriot PAC-3 missiles, and other advanced US systems. The 2023 Washington Declaration established a new Nuclear Consultative Group.
incidents: Periodic burden-sharing negotiations over host nation support costs
US pressure on Korea to decouple from China in semiconductor supply chains

financial.summary: Deep bilateral investment ties. Korean FDI in the US has surged following the Inflation Reduction Act — Samsung (Texas chip fab), LG Energy Solution, SK Battery America, and Hyundai (Georgia EV plant) have committed hundreds of billions to US manufacturing. US FDI in Korea includes major technology, financial, and consumer companies. Korean holdings of US Treasuries are significant.

energy.summary: The US is one of Korea's largest LNG suppliers. US LNG exports (primarily from Gulf Coast terminals) to Korea have grown dramatically. Korea's KOGAS and private utilities hold long-term US LNG contracts. The energy relationship aligns with broader security and supply diversification goals.

scientific.summary: The US-Korea science and technology relationship is among the most productive globally. Cooperation spans semiconductor technology (Chip4 alliance), AI, space (KARI-NASA cooperation), quantum computing, biotech, and defense science. The Chip4 semiconductor alliance (US, Korea, Japan, Taiwan) coordinates allied chip security. Both countries share classified defense research. Korean students are among the largest groups in US graduate science programs.
